risk_of_bias.support_text: Comment:inadequate description of the generation of the randomised sequence Quote:"on completion of baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops" (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
Comment:method of concealment not reported by study authors

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Comment:performance bias due to lack of blinding, and therefore likely knowledge of the allocated intervention by participants and personnel during the study, which may have influenced subjective study outcomes (i.e. self‐report measures)

Comment:detection bias due to knowledge of the allocated intervention by outcome assessors, and outcome measurement is likely to be influenced by lack of blinding (pre‐post self‐report measures tied closely to intervention purpose)
Comment:1 participant was excluded from analyses due to inability to complete the postintervention measure; however, authors do not report the participant's allocation condition (Alvarez 2010, p 212)
Comment:study protocol not available; however, the published report,Alvarez 2010, and unpublished report,Alvarez 2008, are consistent

Comment:additional potential sources of bias related to the specific study design have been identified Reliability of outcome measures (measurement bias) High risk Comment:low internal consistency for eligible outcomes measured by the Knowledge of Child Maltreatment Reporting Laws Inventory (α = 0.18) and Recognition and Intent to Report Child Maltreatment Inventory (α = 0.10); however, test‐retest reliability was acceptable for both measures (r = 0.88 for both) (Alvarez 2008, p 288) Group comparability (selection bias) Unclear risk Comment:no statistically significant differences between conditions at baseline on demographic variables, which is supported by report of data between conditions and formal statistical testing. No formal assessment of comparability on primary and secondary outcomes at baseline. Across conditions at baseline, mean scores < 1‐point difference. The Knowledge of Child Maltreatment Reporting Laws Inventory had a range of 0 to 15, and the Recognition and Intent to Report Child Maltreatment Inventory had a range of 0 to 48 (Alvarez 2010, p 214). Contamination (contamination bias) Unclear risk Comment:unclear whether control and experimental participants worked in the same setting, thereby potentially leading to contaminationDubowitz 1991

characteristics.participants:
Location:Nevada, USA
Setting:not reported
Sample size calculation:not reported
Sample size:55 mental health professionals with a Bachelor’s level degree or higher and graduate students in mental health programs (i.e. psychology, educational psychology, counselling, social work) (Alvarez 2010, p 213); intervention group n and control group n not reported
Mean age (SD):(i) intervention group = 40 (10.9) years, (ii) control group = 36.6 (12.4) years (Alvarez 2010, p 213)
Gender:(i) intervention group = 88.9% women, (ii) control group = 77.8% women (Alvarez 2010, p 213)
Race/ethnicity:(i) intervention group = 80.8% Caucasian (understood to be white), (ii) control group = 74.1% Caucasian (understood to be white) (Alvarez 2010, p 213)
Years of experience:not reported
Previous child protection training:not reported
Previous experience with child maltreatment reporting:(i) intervention group = 51.9% yes, (ii) control group = 59.3% yes (Alvarez 2010, p 213)
Baseline equivalence:authors report no statistical differences between participants in the intervention and control conditions prior to receiving training (Alvarez 2010, p 213)
characteristics.interventions:
Name:child maltreatment reporting workshop (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
Contents:(i) how to involve caregivers in the reporting process, (ii) systematic dissemination of state and federal laws relevant to reporting suspected child maltreatment, (iii) common indicators of child maltreatment, (iv) common misconceptions resulting in failure to report suspected child maltreatment (Alvarez 2010, p 213), (v) empirically supported procedures to assist in making a child maltreatment report (Alvarez 2010, p 215), and (vi) prevalence rates of child maltreatment (Alvarez 2008, pp 51‐2)
Processes and teaching methods:(i) presentation of training agenda, (ii) presentation of information, (iii) modelling in videotaped role‐play scenario, (iv) practice of techniques in role plays, (v) group discussion, and (vi) questions and answers (Alvarez 2008, p 51‐2)
Delivery mode:face‐to‐face workshop
Trainers and qualifications:non‐licensed graduate students at Master's level enrolled in a clinical psychology doctoral programme (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
Duration:2 hours (Alvarez 2008, p 56)
Intensity:not reported
Intervention integrity:workshop facilitators and blinded independent raters completed protocol checklists (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
Comparison condition:alternative training (cultural sensitivity workshop) (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
characteristics.outcomes:
Eligible measures (outcome domain)
Recognition and intention to report suspected child maltreatment (primary outcome: number of reported cases of child abuse and neglect, measured subjectively by participant responses to vignettes), comprising 8 vignettes with response options on a 7‐point Likert‐type scale (Alvarez 2010, p 214)
Knowledge of child maltreatment reporting laws (secondary outcome: knowledge of reporting duty, processes, procedures), comprising 15 items with 4 multiple‐choice response options for each item (Alvarez 2010, p 214)
Ineligible measures (reason):clinical expertise in reporting suspected child maltreatment (measures skills in safeguarding therapeutic relationships; not listed in protocol for this review), comprising 15 items with multiple‐choice response options for each item (Alvarez 2010, p 214)
Timing of outcome assessment:pre‐test (immediately before training), post‐test (immediately after training) (Alvarez 2010, p 215)
characteristics.notes:
Funding:National Institute on Drug Abuse (1R01DA020548‐01A1)
Author contact:yes

sections.text:
Experts have long stressed the need to adequately train professionals to report suspected child maltreatment (Beck, Ogloff, & Corbishley, 1994; Kenny, 2001). Unfortunately, there has not been widespread dissemination of training programs to assist professionals in mandated reporting of suspected child maltreatment. For instance, of the 142 American Psychological Association (APA)-accredited doctoral programs, only 11% were found to offer courses specific to child maltreatment, with fewer still including evidence-based procedures involved in the reporting suspected child maltreatment (Champion, Shipman, Bonner, Hensley, & Howe, 2003).

The current article reports the development of a training program to assist professionals in effectively reporting suspected child maltreatment. Along these lines, we first underscore child maltreatment reporting practices and techniques that have been promoted based on clinical lore or results found in uncontrolled trials (i.e., did not involve random assignment of participants to experimental conditions or case trials involving multiple baseline or withdrawal experimental designs). We then review a training program that is specific to child maltreatment reporting and found to be efficacious in a single-case multiple baseline trial. This program incorporates many of the practices and techniques that have been pioneered in uncontrolled trials. However, as will be carefully reviewed below, this program lacks important features that are relevant to dissemination, such as visual aides and facilitation of structured opportunities to practice demonstrated skills with peers. Moreover, this program has yet to be examined in a randomized controlled trial. Therefore, the current study was conducted to further develop this intervention by comparing it to an active control intervention that is specific to training professionals to enhance their ethnic cultural sensitivity.

sections.section: Involving Clients in the Reporting Process
sections.text: For some time now, professional groups, such as the American Psychological Association (APA), have endorsed recommendations pertaining to the method of training in reporting suspected child abuse (see Champion et al., 2003). These recommendations have included the employment of interactive exercises involved in practicing the reporting process in realworld scenarios and specifying how to best involve clients when making reports. It makes intuitive sense that reporting suspected child maltreatment may damage the therapeutic relationship or lead to retaliation by the suspected perpetrator. However, this does not appear to be the case. As reported by Berliner (1993), only 4% of clients who are reported for child maltreatment respond with threats or attempts to harm professionals. Moreover, unless immediate harm to the child is expected, excluding the suspected perpetrators from the reporting process may be more damaging if the source of the report is discovered. Others have reported that discussing with primary caregivers why mandated reports of suspected child maltreatment need to occur prior to initiating reports may assist in minimizing participant distrust with those making the reports (Dawes-Knight et al., 2006). Stadler (1989) suggested perpetrators of suspected abuse should be given the option of initiating or witnessing the report. Listening to potential concerns of clients in the earlier stages of the reporting process is reassuring, and Weinstein, Levine, Kogan, Harkavy-Friedman, and Miller (2000) found that outcomes were enhanced when professionals informed clients of their decision to report and carefully reviewed limits of confidentiality with clients during the initial session.

sections.section: Content to Emphasize in Reporting Suspected Child Maltreatment
sections.text: Prior to teaching mandated professionals what to disclose to clients during the reporting process, training consultants should describe how child protective service (CPS) agencies determine whether to accept or “screen out” reports of suspected maltreatment (Pence & Wilson, 1994). It is also important to review the respective State laws governing child maltreatment, expectations of the person receiving the report (Kuest & Winter, 2000), goals involved in the investigation of child maltreatment (Kuest & Winter, 2000), and methods of adjusting the treatment plan following the report (Chamberlain, Krell, & Preis, 1982). In training professionals how to make reports, Taylor and Adelman (1998) recommended first providing clients with an explanation of why the report needs to be made, discussing possible outcomes of the report, and soliciting input regarding how to proceed in the report. When involving clients in the reporting process, role-playing such scenarios permits practice of complicated skill sets that are likely to protect the therapeutic relationship (Strozier et al., 2005). Along these lines, the process of reporting suspected child maltreatment with clients is quite stressful, and role-playing these techniques in workshop settings permits mandated reporters to receive positive feedback from others. Such practice inevitably decreases anxiety when making reports and assists in reducing biases that may negatively affect reporting procedures (Drake, Lee, & Jonson-Reid, 2009; Egu & Weiss, 2003; Jones et al., 2008).

Training programs specific to reporting suspected child maltreatment have been developed consistent with the aforementioned guidelines. However, experimental evaluations of these programs have not involved methods of maintaining or assessing intervention integrity, random assignment of participants to experimental conditions, or multiple baseline or withdrawal experimental designs. Along these lines, Macleod et al. (2003) reported on the results of a child maltreatment workshop specific for medical doctors. The workshop included a review of written materials and question and answer sessions. Postworkshop interviews indicated that the 57 junior medical doctor participants increased their confidence in responding to child maltreatment. However, the investigators failed to examine potential changes in reporting competencies of participants.

McCauley, Jenckes, and McNutt (2003) developed a 35-min training video to address reporting of suspected child maltreatment, including definitions, epidemiology and patient presentation of child maltreatment, and available treatment options. The program included role-playing appropriate therapeutic responses relevant to simulated interpersonal violence scenarios. A total of 120 physicians and 172 additional staff completed pre and postviewing questionnaires, the results of which indicated improvements in the identification of physical indicators of maltreatment. However, there were no significant improvements in knowledge of legal reporting requirements.

The Southern Australia Education Department Mandated Notification Training program includes one day of training designed to increase educators’ awareness of personal variables influencing responses to child maltreatment, child perspective taking ability, recognition of maltreatment, and knowledge of legal reporting requirements and reporting procedures. Hawkins and McCullum (2001a, 2001b) compared 41 educators who had recently completed this training, 73 who had completed the training previously, and 31 controls who had not completed this training program. Educators recently receiving this training indicated an increase in their perceived preparation to report suspected child maltreatment, provided more appropriate responses to hypothetical situations involving child maltreatment, and were more likely to identify sexual maltreatment in vignettes (Hawkins & McCallum, 2001a, 2001b). However, the results are difficult to interpret due to arbitrary assignment of participants to experimental conditions.

The Identification and Reporting of Child Abuse Maltreatment program was developed as a prerequisite for professionals seeking licensure. The program was evaluated in a diverse group of 536 professionals using a pre/post experimental design. Results indicated improvements in professionals’ knowledge about child maltreatment reporting (Reiniger, Robison, & McHugh, 1995). However, 60% of the participants did not complete the postintervention assessment.

Finally, Donohue, Carpin, Alvarez, Ellwood, and Jones (2002) examined skill acquisition in a medical student who was trained to involve nonperpetrating caregivers in the process of reporting suspected child maltreatment. Using multiple baseline methodology, a checklist was developed to assist in the initiation of the reporting process and resolving client upset specific to being notified of the need to report. The checklist was designed to assist in maintaining intervention adherence and its content was supported from the work of other investigators (e.g., Berliner, 1993; Dawes-Knight et al., 2006; Stadler, 1989). Reporters can use the checklist as an in vivo guide during implementation of prescribed protocol, which assists in preventing professionals from modifying or drifting away from evidence-based content due to personal biases that sometimes influence child abuse reporting behavior (see Drake & Zuravin, 1998). Ratings of the participant’s performance in reporting suspected child maltreatment by experts during simulated role-play scenarios indicated significant improvements in skill acquisition. These gains were maintained at a 45-day follow-up session. However, the study involved only one participant, and the curriculum was limited to reporting suspected child maltreatment with a nonperpetrating significant other. Moreover, there was no formalized method of presenting background information about child maltreatment, such as slide shows depicting State and Federal laws and facilitation of structured feedback opportunities from peers.

The current study was conducted to further develop the workshop that was favorably evaluated in the aforementioned study by Donohue et al. (2002). There was essentially no change in the workshop’s content relevant to how to involve caregivers in the reporting process. However, several changes were made to enhance its applicability and ease of administration, including its inclusion of caregivers who are suspected of child maltreatment, development of a Powerpoint slide show to assist in dissemination of workshop content in a group format, use of videotape vignettes to demonstrate successful implementation of workshop content, systematic dissemination of State and Federal laws that are relevant to reporting suspected child maltreatment, presentation of common indicators of child maltreatment, and review of common misconceptions resulting in failure to report suspected child maltreatment.

To assist in determining the application of this training program to students and professionals of diverse backgrounds, a between-groups experimental methodology was used, including standardized training protocols, assessment of the integrity of training, and random assignment of participants to the child abuse reporting workshop or a workshop designed to enhance ethnic cultural sensitivity. Although not a focus in the current study, ethnic cultural sensitivity is an important skill set involved in the provision of therapy (Sue & Lam, 2002). Ethnic cultural sensitivity was chosen as the comparison workshop in this study because it has been shown to enhance perceptions of the clinical skills of interviewers but does not explicitly address issues that are specific to reporting suspected child maltreatment (Donohue, Strada, et al., 2006). Thus, improvements in measures that are relevant to suspected child abuse reporting from pre to postworkshop intervention for participants assigned to a training program specific to suspected child abuse reporting, as compared to participants assigned to training in ethnic cultural sensitivity, would permit the drawing of definitive conclusions. Other control groups were considered. However, the ethnic cultural sensitivity workshop was ultimately chosen due to its appeal in recruiting participants who expressed a desire to learn contemporaneous topics worthy of continuing education credit. It was hypothesized that participants in the child maltreatment reporting workshop condition would evidence (a) significantly greater improvements in knowledge of child maltreatment reporting laws, (b) greater accuracy in the identification of child maltreatment, and (c) greater clinical management of child maltreatment reports than participants in the control condition from pre to postworkshop.

## Participants

The participants were 55 mental health professionals with a bachelor’s level degree or higher and graduate students in mental health programs (i.e., psychology, counseling, social work, and educational psychology). One participant was excluded from the study because this individual was unable to complete the postworkshop assessment. Table 1 shows demographic characteristics of the study sample. Chi-square and analyses of variance (ANOVAs) indicated there were no statistical differences between participants in the two experimental conditions prior to receiving training (all p values > .05).

## Measures

Because there are currently no psychometrically validated measures specifically developed to assess skills in reporting suspected child maltreatment, three measures were piloted in this study including (a) knowledge of child maltreatment reporting laws, (b) recognition and intent to report suspected child maltreatment, and (c) clinical expertise in reporting suspected child maltreatment. These measures were developed using expert consensus, focus groups, and a number of other recommended methods for scale development that are described in more detail in the following sections. We do not report estimates of internal consistency because item content of the measures was heterogeneous, making this form of reliability inappropriate for the current measures (Anastasi & Urbina, 1997; Kline, 2005; Strickland, 1999).

## Measures

### Knowledge of child maltreatment reporting laws

This inventory was developed to measure participants’ knowledge of both State and Federal laws related to child maltreatment. To assist in the initial development of this measure, literature reviews of Federal and Nevada statutes relevant to child maltreatment reporting were independently conducted by two graduate students with experience in child maltreatment reporting. A focus group, facilitated by a moderator who directed the discussion and documented the process (Ritchie & Lewis, 2003), was conducted to determine the initial item pool. Participants in the focus group (i.e., mental health professionals and students) were provided copies of Federal and Nevada revised statutes for individual review during the focus group to identify pertinent content (DeVellis, 2003). Consistent with the recommendations of Ritchie and Lewis (2003), the focus group participants subsequently discussed how the obtained information could be refined to permit item development. Along these lines, participants agreed that approximately 50% of the items should reflect Federal laws and 50% should reflect Nevada statutes. Areas of inclusion were determined to be maltreatment definitions, reporting timelines, reporting procedures, and reporting consequences. After initial item development, a second focus group was conducted to review and refine wording and clarity of the items. Following initial item generation, item stems (i.e., questions) and a multiple choice response format for each item stem (four choices for each item) were reviewed by two CPS investigators to verify correct interpretation of the law. Items that did not result in 100% agreement between CPS professionals were discarded. The resulting inventory included 15 items, each with 4 response options to reduce error while maintaining parsimony (Murphy & Davidshofer, 2001). Scores are derived by adding the number of correct answers (i.e. range = 0–15), with higher scores indicating better performance in knowledge of State and Federal laws. Seven items are specific to Federal legislation and eight items are specific to Nevada Statutes. Item stems query participants on laws specific to child maltreatment reporting, including definitions of maltreatment, mandated reporters, reporting timeline, method of report, immunity, and criminal liability.

## Measures

### Recognition and intent to report suspected child maltreatment

This inventory was empirically developed to assess participants’ ability to accurately report suspected child maltreatment scenarios. Separate literature reviews conducted by two graduate students were used to identify indicators of child maltreatment for review in focus groups. The focus group participants who assisted in determining the knowledge of child maltreatment laws inventory developed the structure and format of items through investigation of existing measures and their input (Johnston, Leung, Fielding, Tin, & Ho, 2003). As a result of these focus groups, an initial pool of 19 vignettes was developed with a minimum of four vignettes addressing each type of maltreatment (i.e., physical abuse, neglect, sexual abuse, and emotional abuse).

The initial pool of 19 scenarios was presented in random order to two independent CPS professionals for the purpose of validation (i.e., whether the scenarios reflected sufficient indication of child maltreatment to warrant a report or not). The professionals were also asked which type of child maltreatment was reflected in each of the scenarios. Scenarios with 100% agreement between professionals were considered for inclusion. In the event that more than the necessary two scenarios for a given maltreatment type were selected through this process, scenario inclusion was determined by random selection. Thus, the resulting inventory consists of eight child maltreatment vignettes. For each of the four types of child maltreatment, one scenario necessitated a mandated report and one scenario did not necessitate a mandated report. Respondents indicated their likelihood of reporting suspected child maltreatment from highly unlikely to highly likely on a 7-point Likerttype scale, with greater scores indicating greater likelihood of reporting maltreatment. As half of the scenarios did not necessitate a mandated report, responses to these items were reverse scored (e.g. 1 = 7) so that all scores would reflect accuracy in distinguishing between scenarios necessitating or not necessitating a report and subsequent indication of reporting behavior.

## Measures

### Clinical expertise in reporting suspected child maltreatment

This inventory was developed to determine participants’ understanding of information relevant to safeguarding the therapeutic relationship when making a report. Consistent with the preceding inventories, content was obtained in literature reviews and the resulting content was reviewed by the aforementioned focus group to generate item stems and response formats (Ritchie & Lewis, 2003). An initial sample of 20 items was generated with a multiple choice response format. Four response alternatives were provided to limit error (Murphy & Davidshofer, 2001). Following initial sample development, two CPS professionals independently reviewed the items for accuracy and clarity. Items with 100% agreement between professionals were considered for inclusion in the inventory. Items deemed redundant were excluded, resulting in a 15-item scale. Scores are derived by adding the number of correct answers (i.e., range = 0–15), with higher scores indicating greater clinical expertise in reporting suspected child maltreatment.

## Procedure

Mental health professionals were informed of the study through e-mails, flyer postings, and verbal correspondence with administrators and employees in mental health clinics (N = 4), hospitals (N = 2), and government agencies (N = 2). Potential participants were informed of the nature and purpose of the study and encouraged to notify other mental health professionals of the study. Graduate students were invited to participate through e-mail and listserves, flyer postings, and course announcements at a local university.

On arriving for the workshops, participants were instructed to complete informed consent and baseline outcome measures. Measures were presented to each participant in random order to minimize order effects. On completion of the baseline measures, participants were randomly assigned to one of two workshops (i.e., suspected child maltreatment reporting and ethnic cultural sensitivity). Workshops were facilitated by nonlicensed graduate students at the master’s level, who were enrolled in a clinical psychology doctoral program. These facilitators used standardized agendas and protocol checklists depicting relevant content areas to be reviewed during the workshop. After completion of the workshops, participants were readministered the outcome measures in random order. Graduate students received a certificate of completion and licensed mental health professionals received three credits of continuing education for their participation in this study. The study was approved by the Institutional Review Board of the host institution, and participants were provided written informed consent prior to completing any study procedures.

## Workshop Conditions

### Child maltreatment reporting workshop

A Powerpoint presentation was used to present definitions, prevalence rates, legal reporting requirements as set forth by Nevada statutes (NRS 432b) and Federal legislation, indicators of child maltreatment, and empirically supported procedures to assist in making a child maltreatment report. The facilitator reviewed prescribed skills involved in the reporting process, including a videotaped role-play scenario in which a therapist enacted these skills with a client. The videotape was occasionally paused between skill sets to permit participant discussion. At the conclusion of the video, participants were asked what they liked about the therapist’s methods and how they could use the information to report suspected child maltreatment. The discussion format provided an opportunity to learn how personal biases affect reporting behavior. This format also assisted in disconfirming erroneous assumptions through group feedback. Participants were then divided into pairs and instructed to role-play the techniques presented in the videotape using protocol checklists that depicted specific steps involved in making reports of suspected child maltreatment. Whenever possible, facilitators led participants in discussing the reporting process in response to prescribed case examples.

## Workshop Conditions

### Ethnic cultural sensitivity in therapy workshop

The facilitator presented information from published literature about the importance of considering ethnic cultural sensitivity in the therapeutic process and discussed limitations involved in teaching mental health professionals to be ethnically sensitive in therapeutic situations. Participants were presented with an explanation of a behavioral approach to demonstrating ethnic cultural sensitivity. The Semistructured Interview for Consideration of Ethnic Culture in Therapy (SSICECTS) and Consideration of Ethnic Culture in Therapy Scale (CECTS) developed by Donohue, Strada, et al. (2006) were briefly described, including their psychometric support. The facilitator presented a videotaped role-play scenario depicting a therapist administering the CECTS and subsequently using the derived information to initiate the SSICECTS. The facilitator periodically paused the video and prompted discussion by asking, “How can you make this work for you?” Participants were provided with the CECTS and instructed to complete the measure. Participants were then divided into pairs and instructed to role-play the techniques that were modeled in the video. The participants’ experiences in the role-play and clinical utility of the CECTS and SSICECTS were discussed.

## Intervention Integrity

Protocol adherence for the intervention groups was assessed using a percentage agreement method (Donohue, Allen, Maurer, Ozols, & DeStefano, 2004) in which presenters put a check mark next to each prescribed protocol step completed in their respective workshop. Blind independent raters observed the workshops and indicated on separate protocol checklists whether the facilitators completed each task. Protocol checklists completed by the facilitators and independent raters were compared. The facilitators of both workshops indicated that they completed all prescribed intervention tasks, and the blind raters agreed these steps were completed. Therefore, workshop presenters were reliably assessed to implement workshop protocol as prescribed.

## Response to Training

Separate 2 X 2 mixed model ANOVAs were used to evaluate each of the three hypotheses. In these ANOVAs, workshop condition (child maltreatment reporting or ethnic cultural sensitivity in therapy) was the independent variable, assessment was the repeated measure (pretest and posttest), and responses to each of the three instruments were the dependent variables. Table 2 provides pre and posttest means and standard deviations for participants in both workshop conditions as well as results of the ANOVAs. Significant interaction effects were present for each ANOVA (see Table 2). Consistent with the study hypotheses, interaction effects indicated that when compared to the pretest and posttest scores (same vignettes used pre and postworkshop) of participants in the Ethnic Cultural Sensitivity in therapy workshop condition, participants in the suspected Child Maltreatment Reporting workshop condition demonstrated greater improvement in (a) knowledge of child maltreatment laws; (b) differentiating scenarios that necessitate a report from those that do not necessitate a report; and (c) clinical skills relevant to reporting suspected child maltreatment.

Examination of the descriptive data presented in Table 2 indicates that participants in the two workshops had essentially the same scores at pretest. The scores of both groups indicated that all participants were well informed regarding each of the domains, which might be anticipated based on their educational and occupational experiences. Despite their relatively high scores at pretest, the aforementioned significant interaction effect was present from the Child Maltreatment Reporting workshop. Effect sizes indicated moderate effects, even though mean differences from pre to posttest were small. Ferguson (2009) warns against a “quixotic quest for power to demonstrate any difference as statistically significant without considering whether small differences are meaningful” (p. 532). In the current instance, this “quixotic quest” was avoided, as the increase in test performance for participants in the Child Maltreatment Reporting workshop suggest that even well-educated consumers can increase their knowledge of incidents of suspected child maltreatment, which may have life and death consequences for child victims.

Despite longstanding support for the need to develop training programs specific to the reporting of suspected child maltreatment, to our knowledge this study represents the first randomized controlled evaluation of such a method in mental health professionals. Results indicated that participants who were assigned to this training program improved their knowledge of State and Federal laws, were able to identify child maltreatment scenarios, and evidenced knowledge of requisite clinical skills relevant to reporting suspected child maltreatment more than participants who were randomly assigned to a control workshop focusing on ethnic cultural sensitivity. This improvement was present despite the relatively high level of knowledge in both groups, so that even relatively small changes in mean scores from pre to posttest in the Child Maltreatment Reporting workshop participants resulted in significant and, maybe more importantly, moderate intervention effects. The improvement noted in the Child Maltreatment Reporting workshop participants provides initial support for the utility and promise of the training program, and much stronger effects are anticipated if applied in groups that have less knowledge regarding maltreatment reporting laws and procedures. Although these positive results were consistent with our earlier pilot study (Donohue et al., 2002), it should be mentioned that it is difficult to interpret the extent to which the obtained improvements in child maltreatment identification according to written scenarios influence “real-world” reporting practices. As mentioned before, the change in score from pre to posttest found in the child maltreatment reporting condition were less than what we would have expected, which perhaps was due to use of self-report measures with limited psychometric support, and this possibility could not be directly evaluated in the current study. Nevertheless, the current study builds on previous studies and provides a strong base in the establishment of training programs that are specific to reporting suspected child maltreatment. For instance, this workshop is easy to implement and highly transportable, as child maltreatment laws are presented in simplified terminology, and standardized prompts are used to solicit discussion. Discussions assist in tailoring the material to accommodate particular audiences, and are especially important because laws are often unclear and professional dialogue permits opportunities to reflect differing perspectives, enhance interpretive ideas, and recall information. As mentioned earlier, the discussion format also permits group feedback about erroneous assumptions in reporting suspected child maltreatment due to personal biases. Finally, although originally developed for individual training of medical professionals (Donohue et al., 2002), the training curriculum can be easily implemented in group settings, and appears to be appropriate for use in students and professionals based on the current findings.

## Limitations and Future Directions

The current study sample involved a relatively diverse group of students and professionals to assist in testing the training curriculum’s feasibility in this early stage of program development. Although workshop discussions appeared to indicate training was applicable to these participants, the sample size did not permit examination of the degree to which level of training or professional background may have influenced study results and potential selection biases were not examined. Therefore, future outcome studies will need to be conducted with sufficient power to determine whether the developed training curriculum in this study is differentially effective in various professional and student subgroups. These studies will also assist in parsimoniously determining which subcomponents of training to emphasize for particular subgroups. For instance, it may be determined that teachers need training in clinical skills more so than psychologists. Finally, future outcome studies will need to include measures that assess skill acquisition in reporting suspected child maltreatment, such as the role-play assessment measure used in Donohue et al. (2002) or number of suspected child maltreatment reports made. To assist in dissemination and further evaluation of the developed workshop, the Powerpoint presentation, screening measures, and all protocols are freely available from the corresponding author.

Funding

The authors disclosed receipt of the following financial support for the research and/or authorship of this article: This work was partially supported by the National Institute on Drug Abuse (1R01DA020548-01A1).

review_title: Child protection training for professionals to improve reporting of child abuse and neglect
